summary: The author developed a 2.5D pipeline for aneurysm detection that combines skull cropping, vessel segmentation, 2D frame-level classification, and sequence-based aggregation. The core innovation lies in heavily optimizing 2D CNNs with clever frame sampling, relative position encoding, and custom pooling to preserve left/right spatial information. Key techniques like using adjacent frames as channels, leveraging slice spacing for sampling, and refining localizer labels significantly boosted performance. The pipeline ultimately achieved a 6th place finish with a CV score of 0.895.
extensive_summary:
## Pipeline Architecture
The solution employs a 2.5D pipeline consisting of four main stages: skull cropping, vessel segmentation (training-only), 2D aneurysm frame-level classification, and sequence-based aggregation. The pipeline was inspired by a previous 2nd place solution but required substantial adjustments to achieve competitive performance.

## Skull Cropping & Vessel Segmentation
Skull cropping utilizes a simple 3D-Unet trained on open-source data to ensure uniformity across orientations. Vessel segmentation, which only supports axial data, is more challenging and relies on position encoding to convey left/right information, along with mask dilation and Dice loss to address class imbalance. This stage is strictly used during training to sample high-quality negative frames for the 2D classifier.

## 2D Classification & Optimization
The heavy lifting is done by 2D-CNN models (`coatnet_rmlp_2_rw_384` and `maxvit_rmlp_base_rw_384`). The Relative Position Encoding MLP (rmlp) is critical for distinguishing the 13 classes, and a custom pooling layer preserves left/right spatial information before the logits layer. The models process coronal and axial data, with sagittal stacks converted to axial during inference.

## Frame Sampling & Augmentations
Performance gains of approximately +0.01 CV each were achieved through several targeted techniques: fixed-ratio ROI cropping to focus on aneurysm-rich areas, strong ShiftScaleRotate and color augmentations, Cutmix/Mixup, horizontal flips with corresponding target flips, and feeding two adjacent frames as a 3-channel input. Slice spacing information is leveraged to sample adjacent frames further apart when spacing is small. Additionally, manually refined localizer labels enabled more accurate positive frame sampling.

## Sequence Aggregation & Inference Optimization
Initial max aggregation of 2D predictions yielded a 0.87 CV score. Adding a custom sequence model pushed this to 0.88, and a 3-model ensemble reached 0.895 CV. Inference time was reduced by capping the maximum number of frames per modality and subsampling frames for larger stacks (halving for >64 frames, quartering for >128), relying on the 3-frame input window to preserve context. The authors note that a simpler 2-model pipeline would have been faster and nearly as effective, but opted for the 3-model version due to robustness and CV-LB alignment.
competition_flow: Raw CT data is cropped via skull and vessel segmentation masks, sampled into 2D frames, classified by optimized CNNs, aggregated via a sequence model, and ensembled for the final submission.
data_processing: inference frame subsampling (halved for >64, quartered for >128)
ensembling: A 3-model ensemble of the 2D CNNs and sequence model was used, with inference optimized by capping and subsampling frames per modality.
insights: Relative position encoding (rmlp) is crucial for distinguishing the 13 classes in 2D frame classification.
Custom pooling that preserves left/right spatial information prevents feature loss before the logits layer.
Leveraging slice spacing information allows for more effective frame sampling when spacing is small.
Manually refining localizer labels significantly improves positive frame sampling accuracy.
critical_findings: Using a simpler 2-model pipeline would have run in 7 hours and likely been sufficient, as CV improvements from the 3rd model were marginal (<0.01).
Sagittal stacks were ignored during training but converted to axial during inference, showing that orientation normalization can salvage data without retraining.
what_did_not_work: Training a vessel crop classification model performed poorly and was abandoned.
